text:
Jobseekers, this one’s for you.
 Every application, every interview, every follow-up… the pressure is immense.
 And I know what you're thinking: Am I not good enough? 
 But let me tell you, this isn’t about you or your skills. It’s about a broken system where 60% of applicants never hear back. 
 Your mental health is not worth sacrificing for a system that doesn’t acknowledge your worth. 
 Please remember, taking care of yourself is the real priority. 
 Your dream job will come, but for now, breathe. 🌻
Looking for jobs on LinkedIn is like online dating: Full of promises, but in the end, you’re just left ghosted.

LinkedIn scams be like: 'Congratulations, you've been selected for a role you didn’t even apply for!' 
 The catch? Pay Rs. 50,000 for the honor.

sapne dekhna achi baat hai,
lekin job ka sapna dekh ke 'interested' likhna,
yeh toh achi baat nahi hai na?

Next time when I'll be reading some LinkedIn Influencer's story, I am starting from the last line.
If there's a link attached to it, it's most probably a fake one.
Saves me time!

Every time I poured my heart into 5-6 rounds of interviews and faced rejection, it felt like a punch in the gut. The sleepless nights, the endless preparation, all for nothing.

But looking back, I realize it wasn’t nothing. It was the Universe’s way of saying, “Not this one, something better is on the way.”

Every single time, I’ve been shown why that rejection happened.

Doors I thought I wanted to walk through were shut, only to have the right ones swing open.

The kind that aligned with my growth, my values, and my happiness.

At first, it stung. It hurt deeply. But now, when things don’t go as planned, I don’t panic.

I don’t question my worth. I sit back, breathe, and trust. The Universe knows.

I know there's another plan waiting. Something bigger, better, and just for me.

To anyone feeling the weight of rejection: trust that the closed doors are protecting you from something you can’t see right now.

Your path is being cleared for something even more beautiful.

To everyone who's still looking for a job...

I see you. I feel you. 💔

Every rejection email feels like a punch in the gut, and every 'We'll get back to you' sounds more like 'You'll never hear from us.'

But I want you to know, you're not alone in this. 🌸

According to a study, 80% of jobseekers struggle with anxiety and self-doubt during their search. It's normal to feel lost, but it's not the end.

Take breaks, breathe, and remember, this doesn't define you. Your worth is not tied to an offer letter. 💥

Your mental health matters more than any job.

LinkedIn be like:
Day 1: 'I used ChatGPT to write my resignation letter.'
Day 2: '10 lessons AI taught me that changed my life forever.'
Day 3: 'I replaced my entire team with AI agents. Here's what happened.'

Meanwhile the AI:
*Confidently tells you Paris is the capital of Germany*

Bhai, thoda calm down.

Everyone's talking about AI Agents.
But most people still think it's just ChatGPT with a cooler name.

Here's the actual difference:

ChatGPT answers your question.
An AI Agent completes your goal.

ChatGPT: 'Here's how to book a flight.'
AI Agent: Books the flight, sends confirmation, adds it to your calendar.

The shift from respond to act is what makes agents different.

An agent can:
→ Use tools (search, APIs, code execution)
→ Plan multi-step tasks
→ Remember context across sessions
→ Self-correct when something fails

We're not just building chatbots anymore.
We're building systems that do things.

That's the real GenAI wave — and most people are still figuring out basic prompts.

6 months ago, I didn't know what a transformer model was.

I thought 'LLM' was some new programming language.
I Googled 'what is GenAI' at 2 AM hoping no one would find out.

And now? I'm building AI-powered pipelines at work.

Nobody is born knowing this stuff.

The people who look confident on LinkedIn? They Googled the basics too.
They just didn't announce their confusion.

If you're starting your AI journey now and feeling behind — you're not behind.
The field itself is only a few years old.

Start. Be bad at it. Keep going.

The only people who don't catch up are the ones who never start.

GenAI seekhna hai?

Pehle yeh samajh lo:
- ChatGPT use karna ≠ GenAI engineer hona
- 'AI ka future bright hai' sunna ≠ AI seekh lena
- LinkedIn pe 'AI Enthusiast' likhna ≠ kuch bhi pata hona

Aur haan,
Ek online course karke certificate lena ≠ job pakki.

Par iska matlab yeh nahi ki seekhna mushkil hai.

Bas apne aap se honest raho — kya aata hai, kya nahi.
Wahi seedha raasta hai. 🙏

I've heard it a hundred times.

'AI is going to take your job.'

And the first time I heard it, I panicked.

Three sleepless nights. Every article about automation, job loss, the 'future of work.'

Then something shifted.

AI doesn't take jobs. It changes what jobs look like.

The accountant who learned Excel didn't lose to spreadsheets — they became more valuable.
The designer who learned Figma didn't lose to software — they moved faster.

The people actually at risk aren't the ones AI can replace.
They're the ones who refuse to learn how to use it.

You don't have to become a data scientist.
You just have to stop being afraid of the tool.

Pick one AI tool this week. Use it badly. Use it again.

That's it. That's the whole strategy.

Nobody teaches you this in college.

But knowing how to talk to an AI is a real skill now.

It's called Prompt Engineering. And no, it's not complicated.

Bad prompt: 'Write me an email.'
Good prompt: 'Write a professional follow-up email to a hiring manager after a second-round interview for a data engineer role. Under 100 words. Tone: warm but concise.'

The difference? Context. Constraints. Goal.

Three things every good prompt needs:
→ What you want (task)
→ What's the context (background)
→ How you want it (format, tone, length)

That's it.

People who say 'AI gives bad outputs' are usually giving it terrible inputs.

Garbage in, garbage out — still true in the GenAI era.

New LinkedIn post format just dropped:

'I built an AI agent in 10 minutes that replaced my entire workflow.

It reads my emails.
Replies to them.
Books my meetings.
Writes my reports.
Makes my chai.

Here are 7 lessons I learned. 🚀'

Sir, your agent hallucinated three meeting times, CCed your ex-manager on a confidential thread, and opened every reply with 'Dear Valued Human.'

But yes — congrats on the 50K impressions. 🙏

People use these words interchangeably.
They don't mean the same thing.

AI (Artificial Intelligence):
Any system that mimics human decision-making. Broad umbrella.

ML (Machine Learning):
AI that learns patterns from data instead of hardcoded rules.

Deep Learning:
ML using layered neural networks. Powers image recognition, speech, etc.

GenAI (Generative AI):
AI that creates new content — text, images, code, audio.
GPT-4, Gemini, Claude = GenAI.

AI Agents:
GenAI systems that don't just respond, but act.
They use tools, plan steps, complete goals autonomously.

You're not 'doing AI' if you're just using ChatGPT.
You're using a GenAI product.

Knowing the difference matters — especially if you're building on top of these systems.

Nobody explained the context window to me clearly.
So let me do it for you.

Imagine you're talking to someone with short-term memory loss.

Every conversation, they start fresh.
They only remember what's written on the notepad in front of them.
Once the notepad is full — the oldest notes disappear.

That notepad? That's the context window.

It holds:
→ Your instructions (system prompt)
→ The conversation so far
→ Documents you've pasted in
→ The AI's previous replies

When your context window fills up:
→ The AI 'forgets' earlier parts of the chat
→ It starts giving inconsistent answers
→ Your agent loses track of what it was doing

This is why your 40-message ChatGPT thread starts going off the rails.
Not a bug. Just a full notepad.

Context window size = how much the model can 'see' at once.
GPT-4o: ~128K tokens
Claude 3.5: ~200K tokens
Gemini 1.5 Pro: ~1M tokens

1 token ≈ 0.75 words, roughly.

When building AI agents — managing context is not optional.
It's the whole game.

Prompt engineering is a skill.
But people teach it wrong.

They give you templates.
'Use this 7-word magic prompt for better results!'

That's not engineering. That's copying.

Real prompt engineering is understanding WHY a model responds the way it does.

Here's the mental model:

An LLM is not a search engine.
It's a next-token prediction machine.
It completes patterns based on everything it was trained on.

So when you write a bad prompt — you're giving it a bad pattern to complete.

Good prompts do 4 things:
→ Set the role clearly ('You are a senior data engineer reviewing pipelines...')
→ Give precise context (what problem, what constraints, what's already tried)
→ Define the output format (bullet list, JSON, 3 sentences max)
→ Add an example if possible (one example beats three paragraphs of instruction)

The last one — most people skip it.
One worked example in your prompt cuts bad outputs by half.

Templates expire. Mental models don't.
Learn the why before the what.

AI Agent banana hai?

Pehle yeh 4 cheezein samajh lo:

1. Brain (LLM)
Yahi decide karta hai kya karna hai.
GPT-4, Claude, Gemini — koi bhi.

2. Tools
Agent akela kuch nahi kar sakta.
Use dena padta hai tools:
→ Web search
→ Code execution
→ API calls
→ File read/write

3. Memory
Short-term: current conversation (context window)
Long-term: vector database mein store karo
Bina memory ke agent har baar bhool jaata hai.

4. Planning
Simple agent ek step karta hai.
Accha agent multi-step plan banata hai.
Kaam fail ho toh retry karta hai.
Khud check karta hai — sahi hua ya nahi.

Yeh chaar cheezein hain toh agent hai.
Sirf ChatGPT ka API call karna — agent nahi hai bhai. 🙏

The biggest mistake people make when building AI agents:

They think the LLM is the product.

It's not. The LLM is the engine.
Your agent is the car.

I've seen engineers spend weeks picking between GPT-4o vs Gemini vs Claude.
Which one is smarter? Which one is cheaper?

And then they build an agent with:
→ No error handling
→ No retry logic
→ No memory between sessions
→ Prompts written in 5 minutes
→ No evaluation of output quality

The model choice matters 20%.
Everything around it matters 80%.

What actually determines agent quality:
→ How well you've defined the task boundary
→ How clean and specific your system prompt is
→ How you handle hallucinations and bad outputs
→ How you store and retrieve memory
→ How you break big tasks into small reliable steps

Stop debating models.
Start building better systems.

RAG samajhna hai? 5 lines mein.

Tumhara LLM trained hai 2023 tak ke data pe.
Use nahi pata tumhara company ka internal document.
Use nahi pata aaj ki news.
Use nahi pata tumhari database ki cheezein.

RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) fix karta hai yeh:

Step 1: Apne documents ko chunks mein todo.
Step 2: Har chunk ko vector mein convert karo (embedding).
Step 3: Vector DB mein store karo. (Pinecone, Weaviate, FAISS)
Step 4: User ka question aaye toh pehle relevant chunks dhundo.
Step 5: Woh chunks context mein daalo, phir LLM se answer lo.

Model nahi badla.
Sirf context badla.

Yahi hai RAG.
Aur yahi hai kyun har production AI agent mein RAG hota hai.

Here's what nobody tells you about AI agents failing in production.

You test it 10 times. Works perfectly.
You deploy it. It breaks on input number 11.

Because LLMs are non-deterministic.
Same input. Different output. Every single time.

This breaks everything you know about software engineering.

In normal code:
if input == X → output is always Y

In LLM-based agents:
if input == X → output is probably Y. Sometimes Z. Occasionally something unhinged.

So what do you actually do?

→ Never trust raw LLM output. Always parse and validate.
→ Use structured outputs (JSON mode, function calling) wherever possible.
→ Add guardrails — check output before it takes any action.
→ Log everything. Every input, every output, every tool call.
→ Build eval sets — a set of known inputs with expected outputs. Run them after every change.

The engineers who scale agents are not the ones who picked the best model.
They're the ones who built the best safety nets.

System prompt likhna ek art hai.

Aur zyaadatar log ise seriously nahi lete.

Yeh woh pehla message hai jo LLM ko milta hai conversation shuru hone se pehle.
Yahi decide karta hai:
→ Agent kaise sochega
→ Kya karega, kya nahi karega
→ Kaise respond karega
→ Edge cases mein kya karein

Bura system prompt:
'You are a helpful assistant. Help the user with their questions.'

Accha system prompt:
→ Role define karo ('You are a document processing agent that extracts structured data from financial reports.')
→ Kya karna hai clearly batao
→ Kya bilkul nahi karna, woh bhi likho (negative instructions)
→ Output format specify karo with example
→ Ambiguous cases mein kya karna hai, woh bhi cover karo

Prompt lamba hone se mat daro.
Vague hone se daro.

System prompt tumhari agent ki reedh ki haddi hai.
Kamzor prompt, kamzor agent. Simple.

Multi-agent systems — overhyped or underrated?

Honest answer: Both. Depends on how you use them.

Here's the idea:
Instead of one agent doing everything — you split the work.

Orchestrator Agent: breaks the task, delegates, combines results.
Research Agent: only does web search and summarization.
Coder Agent: only writes and runs code.
Critic Agent: reviews the output, finds errors.

Why this works:
→ Each agent has a focused, smaller prompt — less confusion
→ Errors are isolated — one agent fails, not everything fails
→ Parallel execution — multiple agents work simultaneously
→ Easier to debug — you know exactly which agent broke

Why this fails when misused:
→ Unnecessary complexity for simple tasks
→ Context passing between agents is hard to manage
→ Costs multiply fast — every agent call costs tokens
→ Debugging agent-to-agent communication is painful

Rule of thumb:
If one well-prompted agent can do it reliably — use one agent.
If the task has genuinely separate domains of work — split it.

Don't build multi-agent because it sounds impressive.
Build it because the problem demands it.

Function calling changed everything about how agents work.

Before it:
You'd ask the LLM to output a JSON.
Hope it does.
Pray the format doesn't break.
Write 50 lines of parsing code for edge cases.

With function calling:
You define a function schema — name, parameters, types.
The LLM decides when to call it and fills the parameters.
You get a clean, structured output every time.

Example:
You define: get_weather(city: string, date: string)
User asks: 'What's the weather in Delhi tomorrow?'
LLM responds: calls get_weather with city='Delhi', date='tomorrow'
Your code executes the actual function.
Result goes back to LLM.
LLM gives final answer.

This is how agents actually take actions in the real world.
Search the web. Query a database. Send an email. Book a meeting.

Not magic. Just function calling + good orchestration.

Once you understand this loop — you understand how 90% of AI agents are built.

Token limit se pareshan ho?

Yeh signs hain ki tumhara context window bhar raha hai:

→ Agent apne pehle ke decisions bhool raha hai
→ Instructions jo tune start mein diye the, woh follow nahi ho rahe
→ Long document processing mein end part miss ho raha hai
→ 'I don't have information about that' — jab tune diya tha

Fix kaise karein:

1. Summarize mat karo — compress karo
Har step ke baad full history nahi chahiye.
Sirf relevant state rakho.

2. Chunking karo smartly
Document ko randomly mat todo.
Semantic boundaries pe todo — paragraph, section, topic.

3. Memory ko externalize karo
Jo zaroori hai woh vector DB mein daalo.
Retrieve karo jab chahiye, pura context mein mat bhar do.

4. System prompt ko lean rakho
500 token ka system prompt aur 500 token ka conversation —
better than 2000 token system prompt and no room for actual work.

Context window management = agent ki efficiency.
Isse ignore kiya toh production mein rona padega. 🙂
